title: Offices of Notaries

narrative.executive_view: Offices of Notaries are not a credible $1–10M EBITDA acquisition screen. The code has no usable government count, its supplied labor and role data are a lawyer-industry proxy, and the actual service is commonly solo, capped-fee work. Remote online notarization preserves a commissioned human but moves customer access, identity infrastructure and workflow ownership to platforms [S2].
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI can automate intake, document classification, appointment scheduling, identity-workflow assistance and closing coordination. It cannot independently perform the statutory notarial act: even remote notarization uses a live commissioned notary and an electronic seal [S3]. The supplied lawyer/paralegal mix would substantially overstate task value for actual notary offices, so role fractions are intentionally low-confidence proxy judgments.
narrative.value_capture: Savings from office administration are hard for a notary operator to retain. Florida, an illustrative large-state legal regime, caps a traditional notarial act at $10 [S1], while RON platforms aggregate demand and provide network notaries on demand [S2]. This means platforms, title/lender buyers and customers can take the benefit; statutory caps constrain the basic act even where travel or platform fees differ.
narrative.adoption_timing: RON is already operational at scale: Proof advertises a 24/7 network and says its platform completed millions of notarizations [S2]. That is not a census of 541120 firms, but it supports a view that effective digital workflow adoption is above 50% for addressable high-volume demand. Accordingly t50 is set to zero; implementation remains uneven among solo mobile notaries.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The market is overwhelmingly unsuitable for the target EBITDA band. Because the deterministic record has n_total=0 and n_band=null, the required fallback uses an honest near-zero estimate: a generous ceiling of 100,000 potential independent operators, $40,000 annual revenue per operator and 20% EBITDA margin imply only $8,000 EBITDA per operator, far below $1M. No observed firm-count source or code-specific financial series was reopened, so this is an estimate rather than false precision.
narrative.terminal_demand: Demand for legally valid witnessing is durable in the near term because remote workflows still require a commissioned notary and live video process [S3]. But the office as a paid seller is pressured: a platform can match a signer to a network notary with very low wait time [S2], making the local office's client relationship and distribution less durable than the statutory act itself.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: All quantitative industry claims are weak because 541120 lacks a usable code-level public series and the supplied role mix is 5411 legal services. Fee rules differ by state; Florida's cap is an illustrative price constraint, not a national average. Mortgage/real-estate transactions are cyclical—MBA forecast 5.8 million mortgage loans in 2026, up from 5.4 million in 2025 [S4]—so demand volume can change without creating firm-level acquisition value.

sources.title: Florida Statutes, section 117.05: notary fee
sources.what_it_supports: Illustrative statutory maximum of $10 for a Florida notarial act; state-specific fee-cap caveat.

sources.title: Proof Notarize product and Notarize Network
sources.what_it_supports: Platform-operated on-demand network, 24/7 access, claimed scale and distribution role in remote notarization.

sources.title: Traditional vs. Remote Online Notarization
sources.what_it_supports: RON uses a live notary, identity checks, a digital seal and the same legal process; platform scope caveat.

sources.title: MBA Forecast: Total Single-Family Mortgage Originations to Increase 8 percent to $2.2 Trillion in 2026
sources.what_it_supports: Mortgage-originations forecast and demand cyclicality context, not notary-industry size.

n_band.rationale: Shown conservative feasibility chain: assume a generous 100,000 potential independent operators, $40,000 annual revenue/operator and 20% EBITDA margin = $8,000 EBITDA/operator; this is below $1M, so the estimated count in the $1–10M EBITDA band is zero. Fee caps [S1] and platform distribution [S2] make this near-zero conclusion directionally more plausible, but neither source observes the assumed population, revenue or margin.
caveats: No SUSB/QCEW code-level count exists in the supplied record.
The 100,000, $40,000 and 20% inputs are deliberately generous estimates, not observed code-level data.
A rare multi-office or title-adjacent business may lie outside this estimate and should not be counted as a typical 541120 office.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: The supplied 5411 legal-services mix is a poor proxy, but platform/RON evidence supports automation of intake and administration while commissioned witnessing remains human.
caveats: Supplied roles are 5411 lawyers, not actual 541120 notary-office staffing.
The result should not be read as an observed notary payroll mix.

role_judgments.rationale: Lawyer proxy: drafting/research assist, but legal judgment and client responsibility remain.
Paralegal proxy: document preparation and retrieval automate with review.
Legal-secretary proxy: scheduling and document routing automate heavily.
General office clerical workflow is automation-friendly.
Routine inquiries and appointment booking automate; escalations remain.
Search and document tasks automate partly, with verification retained.
Managerial planning remains human; reporting support automates.
Unlisted proxy work is mixed administrative/legal support.

pi_dist.rationale: Statutory fee ceilings and platform-mediated distribution constrain an office's retention of administrative savings.
caveats: Florida's cap is illustrative, not national; travel, RON and signing-service fees differ by state and buyer.

pi_moat.rationale: A RON platform can provide vetted network notaries and workflow infrastructure, leaving a standalone office with limited distribution moat.
caveats: Proof is a platform source and does not measure every local notary's client relationship.

t50_years.rationale: Platform-scale RON and 24/7 network supply support an estimate that effective digital workflow adoption for addressable high-volume notarization is already at least 50%.
caveats: Not a census of NAICS 541120 firms; solo mobile work may lag.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: Set above 50% from visible large-scale RON/platform operation, not an observed industry penetration statistic.
caveats: Platform evidence cannot establish a national code-level adoption rate.

historical_analogs.value: Online travel and document-platform aggregation: regulated human execution remains while distribution shifts to networks.
historical_analogs.rationale: RON changes meeting location and workflow while retaining the human legal role, an aggregation rather than full elimination analogue.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Platform take rates and regulation may produce different outcomes.

selection.rationale: No reopened source measures notary-office owner ages; a broad uncertainty placeholder is not credited as observed evidence.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: No reopened source documents a 541120 succession shortage, so none is credited.
caveats: Absence of evidence is not evidence of no succession issue.

active_consolidators.rationale: One platform-led consolidator/network is credited as active, limited to B.
caveats: A network/platform is not necessarily an acquirer of 541120 firms.

buy_mult.rationale: Near-solo, fee-capped and platform-dependent economics warrant a distressed/asset-light purchase multiple assumption.

exit_mult.rationale: No scalable transferable moat is demonstrated, so no exit expansion is assumed.

pricing_structure.value: Per-act state-capped notarial fees plus negotiated mobile, signing-service or platform fees.
pricing_structure.rationale: Florida demonstrates a per-act ceiling; platform networks demonstrate separate distribution and workflow economics.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: State cap, travel fee, RON fee and buyer-negotiated payout vary.
caveats: Florida's $10 cap is illustrative and cannot be generalized as a national price.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: RON platforms evidence mature digital workflow; moderate proxy task automation and t50=0 refer to different channels and do not assume payroll elimination.

terminal_value.justification: Commissioned notarization survives in RON, but a platform can own access, identity workflow and network supply, pressuring the standalone office [S2][S3].

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: The platform and fee evidence is used only for capture, adoption, terminal demand and buyability; legal-services roles are explicitly labeled a proxy for V.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: State fee caps and platform-mediated supply are reflected in pi_dist/pi_moat, not in role replaceability.

dataset_mismatch.description: Labor share is 5411 ancestor data and every supplied occupation is legal-services proxy data, not Offices of Notaries.
sensitivity: A true notary-office mix could have more owner/notary labor and less legal professional payroll, changing V materially.
Fallback n_band is a near-zero feasibility estimate, not a dataset observation.

market_boundary.dominant_subsegment: Standalone/mobile and small office notary services
market_boundary.selection_basis: NAICS title; title/settlement and retail counter economics are excluded.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: A is 10 at zero and declines to 5 at two years.
B remains negligible even at the high end; the investment universe may be absent.
The proxy makes V unsuitable for a precise notary-office claim.

reviewer_note: All listed score-driving URLs were reopened on 2026-07-21. The null n_band fallback intentionally reports a near-zero, highly uncertain result rather than inventing code-level data.
